questions.text: If a wave can be polarized, it means, it is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Longitudinal waves cannot be polarized as their oscillations occur parallel to the direction of wave propagation, limiting their ability to confine to a single plane.
Stationary waves result from the superposition of two waves moving in opposite directions and do not exhibit polarization, as they are essentially standing patterns.
Superimposed waves are formed by the combination of multiple waves. While they can exhibit complex behaviors, polarization is not inherently a characteristic of these waves.
Transverse waves can be polarized as their oscillations occur perpendicular to the direction of wave propagation, allowing them to be confined to a single plane.

questions.explanation: Only transverse waves can be polarised. Longitudinal waves cannot be polarised.Transverse: vibrations perpendicular to the direction of travel of the wave.Longitudinal: vibrations parallel to the direction of travel of the wave.Sound waves are longitudinal so they cannot be polarised.Polarised: the wave vibrations occur in one plane onlyUnpolarised: the wave vibrations occur in all planes perpendicular to the direction of travel.The reason that only transverse waves can be polarised is that their vibrations can potentially occur in all directions perpendicular to the direction of travel. It is therefore possible to confine the vibrations to a single plane. Or put another way an unpolarised transverse wave is a 3D shape, it is therefore possible to confine it to a 2D shape (a plane). The vibrations of a longitudinal wave occur along a single line, it is therefore not possible to confine that to a plane, i.e. a 1D shape cannot be confined within a 2D shape. Similarly, Stationary waves and superimposed waves can not be polarised as they are along a single line.VIDEO EXPLANATION

questions.text: A pipe that is closed at one end and open at the other resonates at a fundamental frequency of 240 Hz. The next lowest/highest frequency it resonates at is most nearly.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: 80 Hz is incorrect because it does not correspond to an odd harmonic multiple of the fundamental frequency in a closed-open pipe.
120 Hz is incorrect because it is the result of dividing the fundamental frequency by 2, which is not applicable for closed-open pipes that resonate at odd harmonics.
480 Hz is incorrect because it is not an odd harmonic of the fundamental frequency.

In a closed-open pipe, also known as a closed-closed tube, the fundamental frequency (first harmonic) is determined by the length of the pipe. The next lowest frequency (second harmonic) occurs when the pipe vibrates in two equal segments, and the next highest frequency (second harmonic) occurs when the pipe vibrates in three equal segments. The fundamental frequency is given as 240 Hz. To find the next lowest/highest frequency, we can use the formula: fn = (2n - 1) * f1 / 4 where: fn is the frequency of the nth harmonic, n is the harmonic number, and f1 is the fundamental frequency. For the next lowest frequency (second harmonic), n = 1: f2 = (2 * 1 - 1) * 240 Hz / 4 = 240 Hz / 4 = 60 Hz For the next highest frequency (third harmonic), n = 3: f3 = (2 * 3 - 1) * 240 Hz / 4 = 480 Hz / 4 = 120 Hz Open-closed pipes only have odd multiples of harmonic so next f is 3x f1. Hence the answer is 720 Hz.

questions.explanation: In a closed-open pipe, also known as a closed-closed tube, the fundamental frequency (first harmonic) is determined by the length of the pipe. The next lowest frequency (second harmonic) occurs when the pipe vibrates in two equal segments, and the next highest frequency (second harmonic) occurs when the pipe vibrates in three equal segments. The fundamental frequency is given as 240 Hz. To find the next lowest/highest frequency, we can use the formula: fn = (2n - 1) * f1 / 4 where: fn is the frequency of the nth harmonic, n is the harmonic number, and f1 is the fundamental frequency. For the next lowest frequency (second harmonic), n = 1: f2 = (2 * 1 - 1) * 240 Hz / 4 = 240 Hz / 4 = 60 Hz For the next highest frequency (third harmonic), n = 3: f3 = (2 * 3 - 1) * 240 Hz / 4 = 480 Hz / 4 = 120 Hz Open-closed pipes only have odd multiples of harmonic so next f is 3x f1. Hence the answer is 720 Hz.

questions.text: The distance between the consecutive wavefronts is equal to:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The distance between two consecutive wavefronts represents the wavelength of the sound wave. The frequency of the wave can be measured by counting the number of wavefronts detected by the observer over some time.
The distance between three consecutive wavefronts equals two wavelengths, not two consecutive wavefronts.
A radius refers to the distance from the center of a circle to its edge. It is unrelated to the concept of wavefronts.

Diameter is a geometric property of a circle, which is defined as the distance between two points on the circle's circumference that pass through its centre. It is the longest chord of a circle, and it passes through the centre of the circle. The diameter of a circle is twice the length of its radius, which is the distance from the centre of the circle to its edge. The diameter is an important property of a circle, as it is used to calculate other geometric properties, such as the area and circumference of the circle.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option A: One wavelength. The distance between consecutive wavefronts in a wave is defined as one wavelength, which is a key characteristic in the study of wave behavior. Option B is incorrect because it describes the distance between three wavefronts, which equals two wavelengths. Option C and Option D are related to geometric properties of circles and do not apply to wavefronts, making them irrelevant in this context.

questions.text: A person vibrates the end of a string sending transverse waves down the string. If the person then doubles the rate at which he vibrates the string while maintaining the same tension, the speed of the waves:
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Is unchanged while the wavelength is halved.
options.explanation: As explained in the correct response for option A, when the person doubles the rate at which he vibrates the string while maintaining the same tension, the speed of the waves on the string remains unchanged. This is because the value inside the square root in the equation for speed (v = √(T/μ)) remains the same since both tension (T) and linear mass density (μ) remain constant. However, the frequency of the waves doubles, which means the wavelength is halved to maintain the relationship between speed, frequency, and wavelength.

options.text: Is unchanged while the wavelength is doubled.
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. As explained earlier, when the frequency of the waves doubles due to the person doubling the rate of vibration while maintaining the same tension, the speed of the waves on the string remains unchanged. However, the wavelength will be halved, not doubled, to maintain the relationship between speed, frequency, and wavelength.

This option is incorrect. If the wavelength is doubled, it means the frequency is halved, not doubled. In this case, the tension (T) in the string is maintained, and the linear mass density (μ) of the string is constant. Since the value inside the square root in the equation for speed (v = √(T/μ)) remains the same, the speed of the waves will not double; it remains unchanged.

options.text: Doubles while the wavelength is halved.
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. When the person doubles the rate at which he vibrates the string while maintaining the same tension, the frequency of the waves also doubles. Since the tension (T) is maintained, and the linear mass density (μ) remains constant, the value inside the square root in the equation for speed (v = √(T/μ)) doubles. As a result, the speed of the waves on the string also doubles. Simultaneously, the wavelength is halved to maintain the relationship between speed, frequency, and wavelength.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is option a) "Is unchanged while the wavelength is halved." When the person doubles the rate at which he vibrates the string while maintaining the same tension, the speed of the waves on the string remains unchanged. The incorrect options are b) Is unchanged while the wavelength is doubled, c) Doubles while the wavelength doubled, and d) Doubles while the wavelength is halved, as they do not accurately describe the changes in speed and wavelength when the frequency is doubled while the tension is maintained.

questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. The speed of sound is approximately 1500 m/s in water, not in metals.
This option is correct. The speed of sound in metals like steel is approximately 5000 m/s, which is significantly higher than in other mediums.
This option is incorrect. The speed of sound is around 330 m/s in air, not in metals.
This option is incorrect. 50 m/s is far too slow for the speed of sound in any common medium.

questions.explanation: Correct option is B. The speed of sound in metal is approximately 5000 meters per second. The speed of sound in solid is 6000 meters per second, while the speed of sound in steel is equal to 5100 meters per second. Another interesting fact about the speed of sound is that sound travels 35 times faster in diamonds than in the air. Sound travels fastest through solids. This is because molecules in a solid medium are much closer together than those in a liquid or gas, allowing sound waves to travel more quickly through it. In fact, sound waves travel over 17 times faster through steel than through air.

options.explanation: This option assumes the wavelength is simply twice the first resonant length, which is incorrect because it overlooks the pattern of resonances in the tube.
This represents half the wavelength of the sound wave, not the full wavelength.
Let λ be the wavelength of stationary sound wave. λ/4 = L1 + e (1) 3/4λ = L2 + e (2) where e is the constant value of end correction. (2) - (1): L2 - L1 = 1/2λ λ = 2(L2- L1)
This incorrectly assumes the wavelength is twice the second resonant length, which does not accurately reflect the relationships in this setup.

At these lengths, the tube supports standing waves.For the first resonance length L1, the standing wave has a node at the water surface and an antinode at the open end, representing 1/4 of the wavelength.For the second resonance length L2, the standing wave represents 3/4 of the wavelength.The difference L2 - L1 equates to half the wavelength, based on the transition from 1/4λ to 3/4λ.Thus, the full wavelength is 2(L2 - L1), making Option C the correct answer.Option A and D incorrectly assume direct proportionality to L1 or L2, while Option B only represents half the wavelength.

questions.text: The diagram shows two transverse pulses moving along a string. One pulse is moving to the right and the second is moving to the left. Both pulses reach point x at the same instant. What would be the resulting motion of point x as the two pulses pass each other?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. The interaction of the pulses does not result in this sequence. The initial upward motion, followed by downward, then upward again should be considered.
This option is incorrect. Although there is an upward and downward movement, the sequence of motion does not conclude with only up then down. It misses the final upward motion as the pulses separate.

S the two pulses through each other a little bit at a time and use superposition to see how the amplitudes add. At first the amplitude jumps up rapidly, then the amplitude moves down as the rightmost negative pulse continues to propagate. At the very end of their passing the amplitude would be all the wave down and then once they pass the point will jump back up to equilibrium.

options.text: There would be no motion, the pulses cancel one another
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. The pulses do not completely cancel each other at point x. Temporary cancellation might occur at certain points, but the overall motion includes upward and downward movements due to partial overlap.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Up, down, up. When two pulses meet on a string, the superposition principle dictates that their displacements add algebraically. Initially, the point moves up as the positive displacement of the first pulse adds with any initial equilibrium position. As the pulses overlap, the displacement at point x might momentarily cancel if one pulse is negative, causing a downward motion. Finally, as the pulses continue to move past each other, the point moves up again as the initial positive pulse continues to affect the displacement at point x. Options A, B, and D do not correctly account for the complete sequence of interactions between the overlapping pulses.

questions.text: Two sources, in phase and a distance d apart, each emit a wave of wavelength λ. See figure below. Which of the choices for the path difference ΔL = L1 - L2 will always produce destructive interference at point P?

By definition when the path difference equals ½ λ or any odd multiple of ½ λ's for sources of the same λ there will be destructive interference.
This option is incorrect because a path difference of 2λ results in constructive interference, as the waves are in phase.

questions.explanation: For destructive interference to occur between two waves originating from in-phase sources, the path difference between them must be an odd multiple of half the wavelength, such as λ/2, 3λ/2, 5λ/2, etc. In this question, the option λ/2 is the simplest odd multiple of half the wavelength, ensuring that the waves are out of phase by 180°, leading to destructive interference at point P. Other options such as d sin θ, (x/L2)d, and 2λ do not satisfy the condition for destructive interference. Specifically, 2λ results in constructive interference because the waves remain in phase.

questions.text: What happens whenever a transverse wave travelling in a denser medium, is reflected from the boundary of the rarer medium?

options.text: The direction of its displacement remains the same

options.text: An incident crest on reflection disappears

options.text: An incident trough on reflection remains a trough

During this reflection, the wave undergoes a 180-degree phase shift, causing the crest of the incident wave to become a trough upon reflection. This phase inversion is characteristic of waves reflecting from a denser medium. In summary, Option C is correct as the incident crest indeed becomes a trough. Options A, B, and D are incorrect because they do not account for the phase inversion that occurs during reflection from a denser medium.

questions.text: In a stationary wave, all particles are:
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: at rest at the same time twice in every period of oscillation
options.explanation: This option is correct. In a stationary wave, all particles reach their equilibrium position and are momentarily at rest twice during each oscillation period: once when the wave is at its maximum positive displacement and once at maximum negative displacement.

options.text: at rest at the same time only once in every period of oscillation
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Particles in a stationary wave come to rest twice during each period: once at their maximum positive displacement and once at their maximum negative displacement.

options.text: never at rest at the same time
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. In a stationary wave, particles are indeed at rest simultaneously twice in each oscillation period, at the nodes.
This option is incorrect. Particles in a stationary wave are at rest at specific times during the oscillation period, namely at the nodes.

questions.explanation: Option A is correct. In a stationary wave, particles oscillate between positions of maximum positive and negative displacements. During each complete cycle of oscillation, every particle in the wave passes through its equilibrium point twice, where it is momentarily at rest. This occurs simultaneously for all particles at the nodes of the wave. Option B is incorrect because particles are at rest twice, not once. Option C is incorrect because particles are at rest at the nodes. Option D is incorrect because particles are indeed at rest at the nodes twice during each period.

questions.text: When a standing wave is set up on a string fixed at both ends, which of the following statements is true?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Sum of the number of antinodes and the number of nodes is always even
options.explanation: A. The sum of the number of antinodes and the number of nodes is not always even. The sum depends on the specific standing wave pattern and the mode of vibration. In some cases, it may be even, but in other cases, it may not.

options.text: Wavelength = length string / number of nodes
options.explanation: B. The wavelength of a standing wave on a string is not equal to the length of the string divided by the number of nodes. The wavelength is determined by the distance between two adjacent nodes or two adjacent antinodes.

options.text: The shape of the string at any instant shows a symmetry about the midpoint of the string
options.explanation: When a standing wave is set up on a string fixed at both ends, the correct statement is that the shape of the string at any instant shows symmetry about the midpoint of the string. This means that the displacement of the string from its equilibrium position is the same on either side of the midpoint.When a standing wave is set up on a string fixed at both ends, the following statement is true:A. The nodes are points of minimum displacement, and the antinodes are points of maximum displacement.In a standing wave on a string fixed at both ends, certain points on the string remain stationary while others oscillate with varying amplitudes. These stationary points are called nodes, and the points with maximum displacement are called antinodes.The standing wave is formed due to the superposition of two waves traveling in opposite directions along the string. The interference of these waves leads to constructive and destructive interference at different points on the string.At the nodes, the two waves interfere destructively, resulting in zero displacement. This means the string does not move at the nodes, and they represent points of minimum displacement.At the antinodes, the two waves interfere constructively, leading to the maximum displacement of the string. The amplitude of the oscillations is highest at the antinodes, making them points of maximum displacement.The standing wave pattern has a fixed pattern of nodes and antinodes, depending on the mode of vibration (fundamental mode or higher harmonics). The specific arrangement of nodes and antinodes on the string is determined by the wavelength of the standing wave and the length of the string between the fixed ends.

options.text: Frequency = number of nodes x fundamental frequency
options.explanation: D. The frequency of a standing wave on a string is not equal to the number of nodes multiplied by the fundamental frequency. The frequency of a standing wave is determined by the tension in the string, the mass per unit length of the string, and the length of the string, but not by the number of nodes.

questions.explanation: A string plucked from 1/2 of the length or any of the further harmonics shows symmetry about it's midpoint, if the wave pattern is divided into two equal parts, then it is observed that they are symmetrical. The frequency of such wave is given by ω/2π.

questions.text: An air pipe is open at one end. A stationary wave is produced in second-harmonic mode. What is the phase difference between the motion of the particles at the closed end of the pipe and at the center of the pipe?

options.explanation: To obtain a phase difference of 0 degrees, we would need to compare the motion of particles between two points that are in the same phase, i.e., between two nodes or between antinodes. So, it is incorrect.
If we compare the motion between the closed end and a point located one-quarter of a wavelength away from the closed end, We will have a 90-degree phase difference. So, it is incorrect.

In an open-ended air pipe, during second harmonic mode, there is an antinode at both ends. There is one full loop and two half loops on its sides.On one end, the wave (blue-colored) is at an angle of 270, while in the center, it is at an angle of 90. The difference between both regions is 180. (270–90). Hence, option C is the correct option.
If we compare the motion between the closed end and a point located three-quarters of a wavelength away from the closed end, We will get a 270-degree phase difference.So, it is incorrect.

In the second harmonic mode, there is a node at the closed end and antinodes both at the open end and at the center of the pipe. The phase difference between the node (closed end) and the antinode (center) is 180 degrees because they are half a wavelength apart. This is why Option C is correct. Options A, B, and D propose incorrect phase differences based on misunderstandings of the wave pattern in the pipe.

questions.text: When a standing wave is set up in a pipe which is open from one end, which of the following statements is true?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Sum of the number of antinodes and the number of nodes is always odd
options.explanation: In a pipe open at one end, standing waves can form due to the reflection of waves at the closed end and the formation of antinodes and nodes along the length of the pipe. An antinode is a point of maximum displacement, while a node is a point of zero displacement. When a standing wave is set up in the pipe, there will always be one node at the closed end (due to fixed boundary conditions) and varying numbers of nodes and antinodes along the length of the pipe, depending on the mode of vibration. In this case, the sum of the number of antinodes and the number of nodes will always be an odd number. This is because there is always one more node than antinode in a standing wave in a pipe open at one end.

options.text: Wavelength = length of the pipe / number of nodes
options.explanation: This formula would be applicable in a different context, such as a string with two fixed ends. In a pipe open at one end, the wavelength is determined by the fundamental frequency and the length of the pipe, not the number of nodes.

options.text: The shape of the wave at any instant shows symmetry about the midpoint of the pipe
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect for a pipe open at one end. Symmetry about the midpoint is characteristic of a standing wave on a string with two fixed ends, not a pipe with one open end.

options.text: Frequency = number of nodes x fundamental frequency
options.explanation: Frequency in a pipe open at one end is determined by the mode of vibration and the length of the pipe. It is not directly proportional to the number of nodes.

questions.explanation: For a standing wave set up in a pipe open at one end, the correct statement is that the sum of the number of antinodes and the number of nodes is always odd. This is because there is always one node at the closed end and an antinode at the open end, with additional nodes and antinodes distributed along the pipe. This results in one more node than antinode, making the sum odd.Options B, C, and D are incorrect because they misapply concepts from other types of wave systems. Option B miscalculates wavelength using nodes, which doesn't apply here. Option C refers to symmetry about the midpoint, which is not applicable for a pipe open at one end. questions.text: In a stationery wave, the phase angle between a node and a consecutive antinode is:

This option is incorrect. A phase angle of 150 degrees does not correspond to any standard phase difference between nodes and antinodes in a stationary wave.

questions.explanation: In a stationary wave, the phase angle between a node and the nearest antinode is 90 degrees, which represents a quarter of a wavelength. This is because a full wavelength is 360 degrees, and dividing it by four gives 90 degrees. The phase angle of 180 degrees corresponds to half a wavelength, occurring between two nodes or two antinodes. The 270-degree phase angle corresponds to three-quarters of a wavelength, while 150 degrees does not represent a standard phase relationship in stationary waves.

questions.text: If a pipe is closed at one end and open at the other, the closed end is a:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: a) Antinode:Option (a) "Antinode" is not the correct answer. An antinode is a point in a standing wave where the displacement is at its maximum. In the context of a pipe closed at one end and open at the other, the antinode occurs at the open end of the pipe, where the displacement of air particles is at its maximum.
Option (b) 'Node' is correct. At the closed end of a pipe, a node is formed because the air molecules cannot vibrate, creating a point of zero displacement.
Option (c) 'Rarefaction' is incorrect. Rarefaction refers to the region in a sound wave where particles are spread apart, resulting in lower pressure. It is not specific to the ends of a pipe.
Option (d) 'Crest' is incorrect. A crest is the highest point of a wave, usually associated with transverse waves, unlike the standing longitudinal waves in pipes.

questions.explanation: In a pipe that is closed at one end and open at the other, the closed end is a node because the air molecules are restricted from vibrating at that point, resulting in zero displacement. Conversely, the open end is an antinode where the air particles have maximum displacement. This is a fundamental characteristic of standing waves in closed pipes. The other options are incorrect because they describe concepts not applicable to the closed end of such a pipe configuration.

questions.text: A small vibrating object on the surface of a ripple tank is the source of waves of frequency 20 Hz and speed 60 cm/s. options.explanation: Point A is located opposite to the direction of the source's motion where the waves are spread out, resulting in a lower frequency. Hence, this option is incorrect.
Point B is neither directly in the path of the source's motion nor in the direction where waves compress the most. Thus, this option is incorrect.

When the source moves to the right, the waves are compressed, which increases the frequency of the waves. The greatest frequency will be observed at the point where the waves are the most compressed, which is point C.
Point D is to the side of the source's motion and does not experience the same compression of waves as point C, making this option incorrect.

questions.explanation: The Doppler Effect describes the change in frequency of a wave in relation to an observer moving relative to the wave source. As the source moves to the right, the waves in front of it compress while those behind it spread out. The frequency is greatest at point C because it is in the direction of the source's motion, where wave compression is maximal, resulting in a higher frequency. Points A, B, and D are either in directions where the waves are not as compressed or are spread out, leading to lower observed frequencies.

questions.text: In Doppler effect, if listener moves towards a stationary source then:

options.text: Observed frequency is greater than original frequency
options.explanation: In the Doppler effect, if the listener moves toward the stationary source, the observed frequency is greater than the original frequency.

options.text: Observed frequency is less than original frequency
options.explanation: This option would be true if the listener were moving away from the stationary source as the distance would increase, causing the waves to be stretched out.

options.text: Observed frequency is equal to original frequency

options.text: Observed frequency is independent of original frequency
options.explanation: This option is not accurate because the observed frequency is directly influenced by the original frequency and the relative motion between the listener and the source.

questions.explanation: The Doppler effect describes how the frequency of waves changes based on the relative motion of the source and observer. When a listener moves towards a stationary source, the observed frequency increases because the waves are compressed, arriving more frequently than if the listener were stationary. Conversely, if the listener moves away, the waves are stretched, decreasing the observed frequency. If there is no relative motion, the observed frequency remains the same. Thus, option A is correct, while the other options describe scenarios not applicable to a listener moving towards a stationary source.

questions.text: The trace shown appeared on an oscilloscope screen with the time-base set to 2.0 ms cm⁻¹. (Image of a sine wave on a graph, with 1 cm indicated on the x-axis) What was the frequency of the signal?

options.explanation: This frequency is incorrect. It assumes a longer period than shown on the oscilloscope screen, which does not match the given 4 cm trace length.

This is the correct answer. The period of the signal is found using the time-base setting: 2.0 ms cm⁻¹ multiplied by the 4 cm trace length gives a total period of 8 ms. The frequency is the inverse of the period: 1/0.008 s = 125 Hz.
This frequency is incorrect. It implies a much shorter period than calculated from the oscilloscope's trace and time-base setting.
This frequency is incorrect. It represents a quarter of the correct period, which does not align with the 4 cm measurement on the screen.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is 125 Hz. First, determine the period of the wave. The oscilloscope's time-base setting is 2.0 ms cm⁻¹, and the wave occupies 4 cm on the screen. This means the period (T) is 2.0 ms/cm × 4 cm = 8 ms (or 0.008 s). To find the frequency (f), use the formula f = 1/T, which gives f = 1/0.008 s = 125 Hz.The other options are incorrect because they either overestimate or underestimate the actual frequency based on the given period derived from the oscilloscope settings and screen measurement.

The wavelength of ultraviolet rays ranges from 10-7 m to 10-8 m, whereas X-rays have much shorter wavelengths, around the order of 10-10 m. Since wavelength is inversely proportional to frequency (as per the equation c = λν, where c is the speed of light, λ is wavelength, and ν is frequency), ultraviolet rays have a lower frequency compared to X-rays. This is why Option D is the correct answer. The other options are incorrect because both ultraviolet rays and X-rays can be diffracted, polarised, and affect photographic plates.

questions.text: Sound wave X has intensity 10¹² times greater than that of sound wave Y. By how much is the amplitude of X greater than the amplitude of Y?

options.explanation: The intensity of a sound wave is proportional to the square of its amplitude. Therefore, if the intensity is 10¹² times greater, then the amplitude is the square root of 10¹², which is 10⁶ times greater.
This option incorrectly calculates the relationship between intensity and amplitude. The correct factor should be the square root of 10¹², which is 10⁶, not 3.16 × 10⁶.
This option is incorrect because it does not correctly represent the square root relationship between intensity and amplitude. The correct factor is 10⁶ times.

This option incorrectly assumes a direct proportional relationship between intensity and amplitude, rather than the square root relationship. The amplitude is not 10¹² times greater, but rather 10⁶ times greater.

questions.explanation: The intensity of a sound wave (I) is proportional to the square of its amplitude (A), i.e., I ∝ A². Given that the intensity of sound wave X is 10¹² times greater than that of sound wave Y, we express this as Iₓ / Iᵧ = 10¹². Applying the proportionality, we have:Iₓ / Iᵧ = (Aₓ / Aᵧ)² = 10¹².Taking the square root of both sides gives Aₓ / Aᵧ = 10⁶, indicating that the amplitude of X is 10⁶ times greater than the amplitude of Y. Thus, the correct answer is 10⁶ times.Option B suggests an incorrect factor of 3.16 × 10⁶ times, possibly from a miscalculation. questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option D: Light can be polarised. Polarisation is the process by which oscillations of a transverse wave are restricted to a single plane. questions.text: Data transmitted along glass-cables is in the form of pulses of monochromatic red light each of duration 2.5 ns.Which of the following is the best estimate of the number of wavelengths in each pulse?

options.explanation: This estimate is too low. The duration of the pulse suggests there are more wavelengths.
This is the correct estimate. Given the duration and speed of light, this number of wavelengths fits the criteria.
This estimate is too high. The duration of the pulse does not support this many wavelengths.
This estimate is vastly too high and not feasible given the parameters of the problem.

questions.explanation: The number of wavelengths in a pulse can be calculated using the relationship between speed, frequency, and wavelength: c = λf, where c is the speed of light (approximately 3 × 108 m/s), λ is the wavelength, and f is the frequency. For monochromatic red light, the typical wavelength is around 700 nm (or 700 × 10-9 m). The frequency f can be found by rearranging the formula: f = c/λ. Substituting the values, f = (3 × 108 m/s) / (700 × 10-9 m) gives approximately 4.29 × 1014 Hz. The number of wavelengths in a pulse of 2.5 ns can be found by multiplying the frequency by the pulse duration: (4.29 × 1014 Hz) × (2.5 × 10-9 s), which results in approximately 1.07 × 106 wavelengths, aligning with Option B. Options A and C underestimate and overestimate the number of wavelengths, respectively, while Option D is not plausible given the context.

questions.text: The intensity of a wave depends on the amplitude. The intensity is also proportional to the square of the frequency. A wave has frequency 3.0 Hz, amplitude 1.5 cm and intensity I. What is the intensity of a similar wave of frequency 6.0 Hz and amplitude 0.5 cm?

questions.text: If a pipe is closed at one end and open at the other, the closed end is a:

options.explanation: An antinode is a point of maximum amplitude in a standing wave. In a closed-open pipe, the open end acts as an antinode because the air particles at the open end can move freely, allowing for maximum displacement and pressure variation. At the open end, the amplitude of the standing wave is at its maximum.

A node is a point of zero amplitude in a standing wave. In a closed-open pipe, the closed end acts as a node because the air particles at the closed end cannot move, resulting in minimal displacement and pressure variation. At the closed end, the amplitude of the standing wave is at its minimum.

Rarefaction refers to the region in a longitudinal wave where the density of the medium is less than its equilibrium value. In the context of a closed-open pipe, rarefaction occurs in the regions of minimum pressure variation, which are typically associated with nodes. Therefore, rarefaction is not directly related to the closed end of the pipe.

A crest is the highest point of a wave. In a standing wave, the points of maximum amplitude, such as antinodes, can be considered as crests. However, the closed end of a closed-open pipe does not act as a crest because it corresponds to a point of minimum displacement and pressure variation.

questions.text: When a standing wave is set up on a string fixed at both ends, which of the following statements is true?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Sum of the number of antinodes and the number of nodes is always even
options.explanation: A. The sum of the number of antinodes and the number of nodes is not always even. The sum depends on the specific standing wave pattern and the mode of vibration. In some cases, it may be even, but in other cases, it may not.

options.text: Wavelength = length string / number of nodes
options.explanation: B. The wavelength of a standing wave on a string is not equal to the length of the string divided by the number of nodes. The wavelength is determined by the distance between two adjacent nodes or two adjacent antinodes.

options.text: The shape of the string at any instant shows a symmetry about the midpoint of the string
options.explanation: When a standing wave is set up on a string fixed at both ends, the correct statement is that the shape of the string at any instant shows symmetry about the midpoint of the string. This means that the displacement of the string from its equilibrium position is the same on either side of the midpoint.When a standing wave is set up on a string fixed at both ends, the following statement is true:A. The nodes are points of minimum displacement, and the antinodes are points of maximum displacement.In a standing wave on a string fixed at both ends, certain points on the string remain stationary while others oscillate with varying amplitudes. These stationary points are called nodes, and the points with maximum displacement are called antinodes.The standing wave is formed due to the superposition of two waves traveling in opposite directions along the string. The interference of these waves leads to constructive and destructive interference at different points on the string.At the nodes, the two waves interfere destructively, resulting in zero displacement. This means the string does not move at the nodes, and they represent points of minimum displacement.At the antinodes, the two waves interfere constructively, leading to the maximum displacement of the string. The amplitude of the oscillations is highest at the antinodes, making them points of maximum displacement.The standing wave pattern has a fixed pattern of nodes and antinodes, depending on the mode of vibration (fundamental mode or higher harmonics). The specific arrangement of nodes and antinodes on the string is determined by the wavelength of the standing wave and the length of the string between the fixed ends.

options.text: Frequency = number of nodes x fundamental frequency
options.explanation: D. The frequency of a standing wave on a string is not equal to the number of nodes multiplied by the fundamental frequency. The frequency of a standing wave is determined by the tension in the string, the mass per unit length of the string, and the length of the string, but not by the number of nodes.

questions.explanation: A string plucked from 1/2 of the length or any of the further harmonics shows symmetry about it's midpoint, if the wave pattern is divided into two equal parts, then it is observed that they are symmetrical. The frequency of such wave is given by ω/2π.

questions.text: Beats can be used to find:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Beats can be used to find the frequency difference between two sound waves or periodic signals. When two waves of slightly different frequencies interfere with each other, they produce a phenomenon known as "beats." Beats are periodic variations in the amplitude (loudness) of the resulting sound wave, which occur due to constructive and destructive interference.The process of beat formation occurs as follows:1. Two sound waves of slightly different frequencies (f₁ and f₂) are superimposed. Let's assume f₁ > f₂.2. The waves with frequencies f₁ and f₂ combine, leading to the formation of a composite wave.3. As the two waves interfere, there are instances where the crests of one wave align with the crests of the other wave, resulting in constructive interference and an increase in amplitude (loudness) of the composite wave.4. Similarly, there are instances where the crests of one wave align with the troughs of the other wave, leading to destructive interference and a decrease in amplitude (loudness) of the composite wave.5. This periodic variation in amplitude creates the phenomenon of beats, where the loudness of the sound alternates between being stronger and weaker.The number of beats per second (B) can be calculated using the formula:B = |f₁ - f₂|where |f₁ - f₂| represents the absolute value of the frequency difference between the two waves.Beats are commonly used in tuning musical instruments. When a musician tunes an instrument, they may produce a reference tone of known frequency and then adjust the instrument until it produces a beat frequency with a tuning fork or another reference tone. When the beat frequency becomes zero (no more periodic variations in loudness), it indicates that the two frequencies are identical, and the instrument is in tune.

Beats do not directly provide information about the amplitude of waves. The amplitude of a wave refers to the maximum displacement of particles in the medium from their equilibrium position. It is typically measured using instruments such as oscilloscopes or by visual observation.

questions.explanation: Applying the phenomenon of beat unknown frequency, ‘n’ of a tuning fork can be measured. A tuning fork of known frequency is chosen by trial and error, which produces beat with this tuning fork. If the known frequency of the tuning fork is n1, then the unknown frequency is almost equal to ‘n’.Now the number of beats in is counted. According to the definition, m = n1 – n. Then a small amount of wax is attached to any arm of the tuning fork to slightly decrease the frequency in order to find whether n1 is greater or smaller than ‘n’. If the number of beats increases then n1 is greater than ‘n’, hence m = n1 – n or, n1 = n + m. But if the number of beats decreases, then m = n – n1 or, n, = n – m. In this way, the unknown frequency of the tuning fork can be determined.

questions.text: In a stationary wave, the distance between a node and consecutive antinode is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: In a stationary wave, the distance between a node and the consecutive antinode is equal to one-fourth (1/4) of the wavelength (λ) of the wave.A stationary wave, also known as a standing wave, is formed by the superposition of two waves of the same frequency and amplitude traveling in opposite directions. The interference of these waves results in nodes (points of minimum displacement) and antinodes (points of maximum displacement) along the wave.The pattern of nodes and antinodes in a stationary wave is fixed and depends on the boundary conditions of the wave medium. For a stationary wave on a string fixed at both ends (like a guitar string or a vibrating rope), the first harmonic or fundamental mode has one node and two antinodes. The distance between a node and its adjacent antinode is one-fourth of the wavelength.Mathematically, the relationship between the wavelength (λ) and the distance between a node and consecutive antinode (d) can be expressed as:d = λ / 4This relationship holds true for any harmonic or mode of vibration in a stationary wave on a string fixed at both ends. For higher harmonics, the number of nodes and antinodes increases, but the distance between a node and consecutive antinode remains one-fourth of the wavelength.

questions.text: A longitudinal wave is moving through a medium. Which of the following statements about the direction of the propagation of the wave and displacement of the medium is true?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Displacement of medium = parallel to the energy transfer Direction of the propagation of wave = parallel to the energy transfer
options.explanation: Longitudinal waves are the waves in which the displacement of the particles/medium is parallel to the direction of propagation of wave/direction of energy transfer.We know that waves transfer energy and a wave will always transfer energy in the direction of its propagation.Hence Option A is the correct option.

options.text: Displacement of medium = parallel to the energy transfer Direction of the propagation of wave = perpendicular to the energy transfer
options.explanation: Displacement of medium = parallel to the energy transfer and Direction of the propagation of wave = perpendicular to the energy transfer is incorrect because this describes a transverse wave, not a longitudinal wave. In a transverse wave, the displacement of the medium is perpendicular to the direction of propagation of the wave.

options.text: Displacement of medium = perpendicular to the energy transfer Direction of the propagation of wave = parallel to the energy transfer
options.explanation: Displacement of medium = perpendicular to the energy transfer and Direction of the propagation of wave = parallel to the energy transfer is also incorrect. This does not describe a longitudinal wave.

options.text: Displacement of medium= perpendicular to the energy transfer Direction of the propagation of wave = perpendicular to the energy transfer
options.explanation: Displacement of medium = perpendicular to the energy transfer and Direction of the propagation of wave = perpendicular to the energy transfer is also incorrect because this describes a different type of wave called a surface wave.

questions.text: TheA cathode-ray oscilloscope (c.r.o.) display shows the waveform produced by an electronic circuit. The c.r.o. time-base is set at 10 ms per division. What is the period of the signal shown?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This answer assumes only 2 divisions for one complete cycle, which is incorrect.
This answer assumes 3 divisions for one complete cycle, which is incorrect.
1 period of wave = 4 divisions. 4 divisions × 10 ms/division = 40 ms, which is correct.
This answer assumes 8 divisions for one complete cycle, which is incorrect.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is 40 ms. The cathode-ray oscilloscope (c.r.o.) shows the waveform, and with the time-base set at 10 ms per division, the period of the signal is determined by counting the number of divisions that make up one complete cycle of the waveform. In this case, one complete cycle spans 4 divisions. Therefore, the period is 4 divisions × 10 ms/division = 40 ms.Other options are incorrect because they assume a different number of divisions per cycle:Option A (20 ms) assumes 2 divisions per cycle.Option B (30 ms) assumes 3 divisions per cycle.Option D (80 ms) assumes 8 divisions per cycle.
